title: Part First
summary: "Paris is always Paris, in spite of the immortal Haussmann, both for those who love it and those who don't," he writes. The author grew up in Paris, the son of an aristocrat. As a child he was fond of almost everyone, but his mother made his life a "ghastly burden" French boys went to school at the Pension Brossard in Paris.

title: Part Second
summary: Barty shared my bed, and we lay awake talking half the night, one couldn't help being jolly! Every ten minutes a sentinel on duty in the court-yard below would intone: "Sentinelles, prenez-garde a vous!" And other sentinels would repeat the cry till it died away in the distance, like an echo.

title: Part Third
summary: Barty Josselin was a lively, irresponsible,irrepressible human animal--always in perfect health and exuberant. He had an immense appetite for food and fun and frolic; like a squirrel, a collie pup, or a kitten. Barty conceived a tender passion for Mlle. Marceline, an uncommonly good-looking woman--a tall blonde with beautiful teeth. Lord Archibald quite fell in love with her.

title: Part Fourth
summary: Lafontaine: Lord Archibald fell in love with a very beautiful and fascinating lady, whose birth and birth and antecedents were alike very unfortunate. The beautiful lady conceived for Barty one of those frantic passions that must lead to somebody's ruin. The story is not new; it's as old as the Pharaoh. And Barty was too proud to defend himself. He married the beautiful lady, and went to the bad altogether.

title: Part Fifth
summary: Barty Josselin was a great genius, a bird of God, who had amused himself by making fools of poor, innocent, humble, wandering minstrels. He had a gift of ease, pathos, rhythm, humor, and charm. No technical teaching in the world can ever match a genuine inheritance. Barty became the most popular young man in the town.

title: Part Sixth
summary: Barty's most successful ditties include "Cours, mon aiguille" and "Avec de bons baisers" Barty and Lady Caroline were great friends, almost inseparable during such hours as the professorate could spare from his duties. They constantly wrote to each other--an intimate correspondence that was only ended by the Father's death.

title: Part Seventh
summary: Barty Josselin was an amateur artist, but his talent was so great that it amounted to real genius. He fell in love with Julia Ironsides, but couldn't sell himself to a woman for gold. Barty wrote a letter to his wife, asking her to marry him. He forgot to say his prayers for the first time in 12 months.

title: Part Eighth
summary: Barty Josselin and his wife, Leah, were considered the handsomest couple in London in 1860. Roberta Jones was Barty's first child with Ganymede Jones, his wife of 20 years. Barty Blaze's wife, Roberta, gave birth to their first child, a boy.

title: Part Ninth
summary: "Barty Josselin's literary style has never been surpassed," writes M. Paroly. "Barty was a slave to his own aesthetic eye and ever-youthful heart" "The beautiful big dogs are more important than all the books in the world, even the master's--even the master himself"

title: Part Tenth
summary: Maurice Josselin lived in Marsfield with his wife, Ida, and their nine children. He recalls the happiest decade of his life, when Barty took over Marsfield. His children are all well known in society and the world, but all but his daughter, Marty. Bobby Maurice was a big strong man, nearly three inches high and over two ounces in weight.
